ideas.The unexamined life is not worth living:
This is the most famous sentence Socrates ever spoke — reported by Plato in the *Apology*, at Socrates' trial, as his defiant answer to the Athenians who would spare his life if he agreed to stop philosophizing. He refuses. He could not, he says, give up questioning himself and others, "for the unexamined life is not worth living for a human being." He would rather die than live without inquiry.

The statement crystallizes Socrates' entire conception of what it is to be human. To live well is not to accumulate wealth, power, or reputation — the things his fellow Athenians chased — but to **care for the soul**, to subject one's beliefs and one's life to relentless rational scrutiny. An unexamined life, drifting on inherited opinions and unexamined desires, is barely a human life at all: it is lived, but not chosen, not understood, not *owned*.

The claim gives philosophy an existential urgency it never loses. Examination is not an academic exercise but the very condition of a worthwhile human existence, and Socrates staked his life on it — accepting execution rather than betray the principle. The sentence became the founding creed of philosophy as a *way of life*, echoed by the Stoics, by Montaigne's essays of self-scrutiny, and by the whole tradition that treats self-knowledge as the highest human task. It also poses a permanent challenge to anyone who hears it: is *my* life examined, or merely lived out on autopilot?
ideas.Knowledge of virtue equals virtue:
One of the "Socratic paradoxes," this doctrine holds that **virtue is knowledge** — that to know what is genuinely good is already to be good, and that no one does wrong willingly. If a person truly grasps that an action is bad for them and for their soul, Socrates argues, they cannot knowingly choose it; all wrongdoing is therefore a kind of **ignorance**, a failure to know what is really good. The wicked are not so much evil as mistaken.

This follows from Socrates' conviction that everyone desires the good — their own genuine flourishing — and that vice always results from confusing *apparent* goods (pleasure, money, power) with real ones. Correct the knowledge, and right action follows automatically. It also implies the "unity of the virtues": courage, temperance, justice, and piety turn out to be, at bottom, one and the same knowledge of good and evil, seen from different angles.

The doctrine has striking consequences. It makes **moral education** a matter of inquiry rather than discipline — if virtue is knowledge, it can in principle be taught, or at least discovered by the learner. And it denies the reality of *akrasia*, weakness of will: for Socrates, no one *really* acts against their better judgment; they only ever act on a mistaken judgment about what is good. Aristotle would take up precisely this point, insisting against Socrates that we can know the good and still fail to do it, because desire and habit — not knowledge alone — govern action. The debate remains alive in moral psychology today.
ideas.The Socratic method (elenchus):
The **elenchus** — Greek for "cross-examination" or "refutation" — is Socrates' signature method, and one of the most influential techniques in the history of thought. Rather than lecturing, Socrates questions. He approaches someone who claims to know what justice, courage, or piety is, professes his own ignorance, and asks them to define the term. Then, through a patient series of further questions, he draws out the implications of their answer until it contradicts something else the person firmly believes — exposing that they did not really know what they claimed to know.

The method is fundamentally **negative** and often ends in *aporia*, a state of puzzled impasse in which the confident answer has collapsed and no replacement has yet been found. But this is not mere destruction. By clearing away false certainty, the elenchus performs what Socrates calls, in the *Theaetetus*, a kind of **intellectual midwifery**: he helps others give birth to their own thoughts and, crucially, brings them to recognize their own ignorance — the necessary first step toward genuine knowledge.

The elenchus embodies Socrates' deepest convictions: that truth must be reached through the interlocutor's own reasoning rather than imposed from outside, that acknowledging ignorance is wiser than false confidence, and that philosophy is a shared, dialogical activity. It founded the **dialectical** tradition, gave Plato the literary form of the dialogue, and survives today in everything from the law-school "Socratic method" to forms of psychotherapy. Kierkegaard, among others, made Socratic irony and indirect questioning central to his own way of doing philosophy.
ideas.I know that I know nothing:
This famous formula captures the paradoxical wisdom at the heart of Socrates' life. In the *Apology*, Socrates recounts that the oracle at Delphi declared no one wiser than he — a pronouncement he found baffling, since he was aware of possessing no wisdom about the greatest things. To test it, he questioned the reputed experts of Athens — politicians, poets, craftsmen — and found that they all *believed* they possessed wisdom about goodness, justice, and the right way to live while in fact possessing none.

Socrates concluded that his own superiority lay precisely in his **awareness of his ignorance**. Others were doubly ignorant: they did not know, and they did not know that they did not know. Socrates at least knew that he did not know, and this honest recognition of the limits of his understanding was the only wisdom available to a human being. Divine wisdom belongs to the god; the best a mortal can do is stop pretending to have it.

This "Socratic ignorance" is neither false modesty nor skepticism for its own sake. It is the very engine of philosophy: only someone who knows they lack wisdom will genuinely seek it, and only the deflation of false certainty makes real inquiry possible. It also carries an ethical edge — the arrogance of imagined knowledge is, for Socrates, the root of much wrongdoing. The stance became a permanent ideal of intellectual humility, echoing through Montaigne's motto "What do I know?" and the entire skeptical tradition that followed.

works.Wrote nothing; preserved by Plato, Xenop:
Socrates is the towering figure who wrote nothing at all — a fact that is itself philosophically significant and the source of one of the great puzzles of scholarship, the "**Socratic problem**." Everything we know of him comes secondhand, through others, and the portraits do not fully agree. He left no treatises, no notes, no written doctrine; his philosophy was lived and spoken, conducted entirely in face-to-face conversation in the streets and gymnasia of Athens.

Three principal sources preserve him, each with its own bias. **Plato**, his greatest disciple, made Socrates the protagonist of almost all his dialogues — but over time "Socrates" plainly becomes the mouthpiece for Plato's own developing philosophy, so scholars struggle to separate the historical man from the character. **Xenophon**, a soldier and historian, offers a more sober, practical, less brilliant Socrates in his *Memorabilia* — thought by some more reliable, by others merely duller. And **Aristophanes**, in his comedy *The Clouds*, caricatures Socrates while he was still alive as a babbling sophist running a "thinkery" — a hostile portrait Socrates himself blames, in the *Apology*, for helping poison the jury against him.

That Socrates wrote nothing may have been a choice consistent with his philosophy. Like his method, truth for him lived in the give-and-take of living dialogue, not in the fixed and defenseless written word — a suspicion of writing that Plato himself would voice in the *Phaedrus*. The result is one of history's great ironies: the most influential philosopher in the Western tradition reaches us only as others chose to remember him.
